raw_case:
Focal necrotizing Histoplasma capsulatum cervical lymphadenitis without evidence of disseminated disease in an immunocompetent patient
Previously healthy Caucasian female in her 30s initially presented for progressively enlarging right-sided cervical lymphadenopathy with associated sore throat. After she completed 2 antibiotic regimens for presumed infection with no relief, a CT Neck with contrast was obtained which demonstrated multiple pathologic lymph nodes, the largest lymph node demonstrating central necrosis. Patient underwent fine needle aspiration with Otolaryngology, as well as core needle biopsy with Interventional Radiology, both of which were nondiagnostic. Subsequently, she underwent excisional lymph node biopsy with Otolaryngology. Pathology reported the presence of numerous budding, oval yeast-like cells in the necrotic areas, diagnostic of Histoplasma capsulatum lymphadenitis. She retrospectively received a CT Chest with contrast to confirm presence of pulmonary or mediastinal disease for disseminated histoplasma infection. Surprisingly, the CT Chest showed no evidence of disseminated disease, indicating a unique case of isolated histoplasma capsulatum cervical lymphadenitis.

Introduction

Histoplasma capsulatum is a fungus commonly found in moist environments and particularly associated with river valleys worldwide. In the United States, the Ohio and Mississippi river valleys, as well as the southeastern states are areas which harbor this fungus, making histoplasmosis the most common endemic mycosis in North America. Histoplasma infection occurs when soil is disturbed, causing the histoplasma conidia to become airborne and inhaled. Though most infections are asymptomatic, a pulmonary granulomatous inflammatory response can occur and incite symptoms similar to pulmonary tuberculosis. Common exposure settings have been attributed to contact with bird and bat excrement in 77% of outbreak settings, and workplace exposures in half of the outbreaks such as chicken coops or construction projects [1].Even though an estimated 60-90% of people in the southeastern US are exposed to Histoplasma in their lifetime, Histoplasmosis is not a nationally reported disease since the average annual incidence is between 1 to 2 cases per 100,000 population. Death rates usually range from 5% to 7% in hospitalized patients in the 14 states that do report histoplasmosis [2]. Significant morbidity and mortality from disseminated infection is seen in infants and in immunocompromised patients.

Case report

A female in her 30s presented to our Emergency Department for complaints of sore throat and “swollen glands” for 2-3 days. She attributed these symptoms to streptococcal pharyngitis which was reported to be circulating at her children's elementary school. She was afebrile in the ED, and physical examination only showed mildly erythematous oropharynx. Rapid strep test was negative, but the patient was prescribed clindamycin 300 mg every 8 hours for 10 days. The antibiotic regimen quickly resolved her pharyngitis symptoms, but the lymphadenopathy continued to grow which prompted her to seek out her Primary Care Provider (PCP) about 3 weeks later. During the appointment, patient reported that she and her family moved to Panama from 2021 to 2023 for military assignment, as well as spending approximately 1 month in California just prior to onset of symptoms. Remote history was significant for her previously living in Mississippi without substantial outdoor exposures other than a period where she participated in composting while in Mississippi. The patient noted that over the years, whenever she experienced an upper respiratory infection, she noted mild swelling of the right neck. Physical exam was benign with no constitutional symptoms and no lung issues to include cough, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, or rales/rhonchi. Laboratory results showed normal white blood cell count, C-reactive protein, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A CT Neck with contrast exam was significant for “multiple pathologic lymph nodes involving the right upper neck. The dominant lymph node is seen at level 3 up to 3.6 cm and appears centrally necrotic (see Fig. 1, Fig. 2). These findings are suspicious for either metastatic disease or atypical infection. ENT consultation recommended.”Approximately 3 weeks after her PCP visit, the patient saw Otolaryngology for evaluation of the right neck lymphadenopathy. Given the concerning imaging characteristics of the right neck lymph nodes, the patient underwent ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration (FNA) with focus on the capsule of the lymph node conglomeration. Pathology results from the FNA were nondiagnostic. Next, the patient was referred to Interventional Radiology (IR) for image-guided core needle biopsy (see Fig. 3, Fig. 4, Fig. 5). Ultrasound guided core needle biopsy of the pathologically enlarged right neck lymph nodes showed “benign lymphoid tissue” and “anucleated squamous cells with necrotic debris and chronic inflammation.” Importantly, potassium hydroxide (KOH) and calcofluor white (CFW) fungal stains were negative.Since the attempts of minimally invasive biopsies were equivocal, the patient underwent direct laryngoscopy without biopsy and right neck excisional biopsy of cystic mass and lymph node approximately 1 month after the IR core biopsy. Pathology results from the excisional biopsy showed numerous “2-4 micrometer budding oval yeast-like cells in areas of necrosis, diagnostic of lymphadenitis caused by Histoplasma capsulatum var. capsulatum” (see Fig. 6).After the unexpected Pathology results of histoplasmosis lymphadenitis, a retrospective CT Chest with contrast was obtained for the patient to assess for pulmonary or mediastinal disease encountered in Histoplasma capsulatum primary infection (See Fig. 7, Fig. 8, Fig. 9). Surprisingly, the CT Chest showed no evidence of axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymphadenopathy, indicating that the patient had extremely rare, isolated cervical lymphadenitis secondary to histoplasmosis.Progressive disseminated necrotizing lymphadenitis without any pulmonary manifestations is a very unusual and rare diagnosis with no clear guidance on management. The patient had positive complement fixation mycelial antibody titers of 1:32 indicating strong presumptive evidence of an active Histoplasma infection but could represent a persistent antibody response from a previous episode of histoplasmosis [3]. Therefore, the patient was started on a 12-week course of itraconazole and, as of this writing, is continuing the prescribed 12-week antifungal regimen.The patient had a follow-up examination with her Infectious Disease physician approximately 5 weeks after the excisional biopsy. She continues to do well with no evidence of recurrent lymphadenitis. Her incision has healed well, and she denies any new lymphadenopathy, swelling around the incision, drainage, erythema, or other complication. She has resumed her normal active lifestyle with no limitations.

Discussion

Patients with histoplasmosis are commonly asymptomatic, but can exhibit low-grade, nonspecific constitutional symptoms which appear days to weeks after exposure. Primary infection occurs when histoplasma spores enter the body through inhalation and lodge in the alveoli. The histoplasma capsulatum spores can incite a pulmonary granulomatous inflammation and mimic tuberculosis with weight loss, chest pain, and hemoptysis. Since the microconidia of Histoplasma enter the body via airborne pathways, pulmonary manifestations are most commonly encountered first and can progress to more serious chronic lung cavitations and disseminated disease. However, invasive and chronic forms of histoplasmosis predominantly occur in immunodeficient patients [4].Progressive disseminated histoplasmosis typically occurs when macrophages transport the unicellular yeast from the lungs to other organ systems. Disseminated histoplasmosis can extensively spread throughout the body and affect the mouth, liver, central nervous system, skin, and adrenal glands. Lymphadenitis secondary to histoplasmosis is commonly seen in the later, more serious, stages of disseminated infection.Isolated necrotizing cervical lymphadenitis due to Histoplasma capsulatum is rare, but limited cases have been reported primarily in immunocompromised individuals of Asian descent. A rare case of necrotizing Histoplasma capsulatum cervical lymphadenitis was reported in an immunocompromised (IFN-γ autoantibodies) patient of African descent who presented with elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and leukocytosis [5].This case is rare in that the patient was immunocompetent, yet she had focal necrotizing cervical lymphadenitis secondary to Histoplasma capsulatum which is chiefly seen with disseminated disease with no evidence of primary pulmonary infection. Furthermore, her benign presentation with no constitutional symptoms or laboratory abnormalities further confounded the diagnosis of histoplasmosis even though she had significant elevated complement fixation mycelial antibodies of 1:32, highly suggestive of active Histoplasma infection.

Learning points


1.Histoplasma capsulatum can cause isolated cervical lymphadenitis in immunocompetent patients, even without pulmonary involvement, highlighting the need to consider it in differential diagnoses.2.Nondiagnostic results from FNA and core biopsies in necrotizing lymphadenitis may require excisional biopsy for definitive diagnosis, especially when infectious etiologies are suspected.3.A thorough clinical history and targeted imaging are essential in diagnosing rare infections like histoplasmosis, particularly in patients with exposure to endemic regions.


Patient consent

Written, informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of the following case report: “Focal necrotizing histoplasma capsulatum cervical lymphadenitis without evidence of disseminated disease in an immunocompetent patient”.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** Previously healthy Caucasian female in her 30s.
- **Chief Complaint:** Progressively enlarging right-sided cervical lymphadenopathy and associated sore throat.
- **History of Present Illness:** Initial sore throat and swollen glands treated with antibiotics (clindamycin) for suspected bacterial pharyngitis, but lymphadenopathy persisted and worsened. 
- **Past Medical History:** None significant.
- **Personal History:** Military family; lived in endemic areas for Histoplasma capsulatum (Mississippi and Panama); no substantial outdoor exposures except for composting in Mississippi.
- **Family History:** None provided.
- **Physical Examination:** Mildly erythematous oropharynx; no constitutional symptoms (e.g., fever, weight loss), no respiratory symptoms (e.g., cough, shortness of breath).
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Laboratory: Normal WBC, CRP, ESR.
  - Radiology:
    - CT Neck with contrast: Multiple pathologic lymph nodes; one node 3.6 cm with central necrosis.
    - Retrospective CT Chest: No pulmonary, mediastinal, or axillary lymphadenopathy (no evidence of disseminated histoplasmosis).
  - Biopsy:
    - FNA and core needle biopsy: Nondiagnostic results, no fungal elements.
    - Excisional biopsy: Definitive diagnosis of Histoplasma capsulatum lymphadenitis based on the presence of budding oval yeast-like cells in necrotic areas.
  - Serology: Positive complement fixation mycelial antibody titers (1:32), suggestive of active or past infection.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Step 1:** **Initial suspicion - Metastatic disease or atypical infection.**
   - Central necrosis of lymph nodes suggested either an infectious etiology or malignancy (e.g., lymphoma, metastatic cancer).
   - Excluded malignancy due to lack of systemic symptoms, normal blood work, and benign pathology findings in initial biopsies.

2. **Step 2:** **Consideration of tuberculosis and atypical infections**
   - Infectious causes of necrotizing lymphadenitis (e.g., Mycobacterium tuberculosis, non-tuberculous mycobacteria, fungal infections) were considered.
   - Negative KOH and fungal stains, normal WBC, CRP, and ESR initially made fungal or mycobacterial infection less likely.
   - Excluded tuberculosis given the lack of pulmonary symptoms, inconclusive pathology, and absence of findings on CT Chest.

3. **Step 3:** **Evaluation for fungal infections.**
   - Persistent lymphadenopathy despite antibiotic treatment raised suspicion for fungal infection.
   - Excisional biopsy revealed Histoplasma capsulatum, confirming fungal etiology.
   - CT Chest ruled out disseminated disease, making this an isolated cervical infection.

4. **Rationale for Exclusion of Disseminated Histoplasmosis:**
   - Lack of systemic symptoms (e.g., fever, weight loss, organ involvement).
   - Normal CT Chest with no evidence of mediastinal involvement confirmed non-disseminated disease.

5. **Final Conclusion:**
   - Diagnosis of focal necrotizing lymphadenitis secondary to Histoplasma capsulatum, with no evidence of pulmonary or disseminated disease. This is rare in immunocompetent individuals.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Focal necrotizing **Histoplasma capsulatum cervical lymphadenitis** without evidence of disseminated disease in an immunocompetent patient. The diagnosis was established via excisional biopsy, and the patient’s clinical history of exposure to endemic areas supported this conclusion.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Focal necrotizing Histoplasma capsulatum cervical lymphadenitis without evidence of disseminated disease.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by reviewing the case. The patient is a previously healthy woman in her 30s with a chief complaint of progressively enlarging right-sided cervical lymphadenopathy and sore throat. She was initially treated with clindamycin for suspected bacterial pharyngitis, but the lymph nodes didn't improve.

First, I need to consider common causes of cervical lymphadenopathy. Bacterial infections like strep throat or staph are typical, but since antibiotics didn't work, maybe it's something else. Viral infections like EBV or CMV could be possibilities, but her labs like WBC, CRP, and ESR are normal, which might not fit an acute viral process. Also, she doesn't have other symptoms like fever or fatigue.

Next, her history of living in endemic areas for Histoplasma capsulatum (Mississippi and Panama) is a big clue. Histoplasmosis is a fungal infection that's common in those regions. Even though she doesn't have respiratory symptoms, the fungus can cause lymphadenitis. The composting exposure in Mississippi might have been a source of fungal spores.

Looking at the imaging: CT neck shows necrotic lymph nodes, which can be seen in infections like TB, cat-scratch disease, or fungal infections. The chest CT was clear, which makes disseminated histoplasmosis less likely. So it's probably localized.

The biopsies are key. The initial FNA and core needle weren't diagnostic, but the excisional biopsy showed budding yeast cells. Histoplasma typically has small, oval yeast forms with narrow-based budding. Even though the initial biopsies didn't find fungi, the excisional biopsy is more definitive. Also, the positive serology (complement fixation titer 1:32) supports an active or past infection. Since the lymph nodes are enlarging and she's symptomatic, it's more likely active.

Other possibilities like lymphoma or other malignancies were probably considered, but the biopsy showing yeast cells points away from cancer. TB would have caseating granulomas, and the AFB stains would be positive if present. No mention of that here.

Putting it all together: Her exposure history, the necrotic lymph nodes on imaging, biopsy findings of yeast cells, and positive serology all point to Histoplasma lymphadenitis. Even without pulmonary involvement, localized lymph node infection can occur. The initial antibiotic treatment failure also supports a non-bacterial cause.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with persistent cervical lymphadenopathy unresponsive to antibiotics, raising suspicion for non-bacterial etiologies. Common differentials include fungal, mycobacterial, or neoplastic causes.  
<step 2> Her residency in Histoplasma-endemic regions (Mississippi, Panama) and composting activity strongly suggest exposure to Histoplasma capsulatum, a known cause of localized lymphadenitis in immunocompetent individuals.  
<step 3> Imaging reveals necrotic cervical lymph nodes without disseminated disease (normal chest CT), aligning with localized histoplasmosis. Normal inflammatory markers (WBC, CRP, ESR) argue against acute bacterial infection.  
<step 4> Biopsy findings of budding yeast-like cells in necrotic areas are pathognomonic for Histoplasma capsulatum. Positive serology (complement fixation titer 1:32) further supports active infection, despite initial nondiagnostic FNA/core biopsy results.  
<step 5> The absence of constitutional symptoms or pulmonary involvement does not exclude histoplasmosis, as isolated lymphadenitis can occur. Other mimics (e.g., tuberculosis, lymphoma) are ruled out by histopathology and lack of systemic findings.  

### Answer:
Histoplasma capsulatum lymphadenitis

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with persistent cervical lymphadenopathy unresponsive to antibiotics, raising suspicion for non-bacterial etiologies. Common differentials include fungal, mycobacterial, or neoplastic causes.

Her residency in Histoplasma-endemic regions (Mississippi, Panama) and composting activity strongly suggest exposure to Histoplasma capsulatum, a known cause of localized lymphadenitis in immunocompetent individuals.

Imaging reveals necrotic cervical lymph nodes without disseminated disease (normal chest CT), aligning with localized histoplasmosis. Normal inflammatory markers (WBC, CRP, ESR) argue against acute bacterial infection.

Biopsy findings of budding yeast-like cells in necrotic areas are pathognomonic for Histoplasma capsulatum. Positive serology (complement fixation titer 1:32) further supports active infection, despite initial nondiagnostic FNA/core biopsy results.

The absence of constitutional symptoms or pulmonary involvement does not exclude histoplasmosis, as isolated lymphadenitis can occur. Other mimics (e.g., tuberculosis, lymphoma) are ruled out by histopathology and lack of systemic findings.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial suspicion - Metastatic disease or atypical infection; central necrosis of lymph nodes suggested either an infectious etiology or malignancy (e.g., lymphoma, metastatic cancer). Excluded malignancy due to lack of systemic symptoms, normal blood work, and benign pathology findings in initial biopsies.

Step 2: Consideration of tuberculosis and atypical infections; infectious causes of necrotizing lymphadenitis (e.g., Mycobacterium tuberculosis, non-tuberculous mycobacteria, fungal infections) were considered. Negative KOH and fungal stains, normal WBC, CRP, and ESR initially made fungal or mycobacterial infection less likely. Excluded tuberculosis given the lack of pulmonary symptoms, inconclusive pathology, and absence of findings on CT Chest.

Step 3: Persistent lymphadenopathy despite antibiotic treatment raised suspicion for fungal infection; excisional biopsy revealed Histoplasma capsulatum, confirming fungal etiology; CT Chest ruled out disseminated disease, making this an isolated cervical infection.

Step 4: Rationale for exclusion of disseminated histoplasmosis; lack of systemic symptoms (e.g., fever, weight loss, organ involvement); normal CT Chest with no evidence of mediastinal involvement confirmed non-disseminated disease.

Step 5: Final conclusion; diagnosis of focal necrotizing lymphadenitis secondary to Histoplasma capsulatum, with no evidence of pulmonary or disseminated disease. This is rare in immunocompetent individuals. Final diagnosis: Focal necrotizing Histoplasma capsulatum cervical lymphadenitis without evidence of disseminated disease in an immunocompetent patient. The diagnosis was established via excisional biopsy, and the patient’s clinical history of exposure to endemic areas supported this conclusion.
